64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67

73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78

80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84

86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...

95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>

97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>

100

102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.

Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. 194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". 200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. 202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.

204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:

209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}

283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]

300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}

306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]

319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}

371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field

387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded

393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference

395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one

402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient

408	  %   (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational). The ambient subquotient route

414	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
415	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
416	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
417	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
418	%  following short exact sequence
419	%  $$
420	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
421	%  $$

423	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	    \leanok
435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}

497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}

574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}

595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}

599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}

601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.

607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}

613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using

For a sheaf \(F\) and a family of opens \(\{U_i\}\) with union \(V\), any family of sections
  \(s_i \in F(U_i)\) agreeing on overlaps glues to a unique \(s \in F(V)\); and a section of \(V\)
  is determined by its restrictions to a cover (\(\mathrm{eq\_of\_locally\_eq'}\)). These discharge
  the existence and uniqueness obligations in the inductive step. The primed companion
  \(\mathrm{existsUnique\_gluing'}\), which presents the same statement for a family of opens whose
  supremum is a fixed open \(V\), is the form consumed by the cover-form descent
  (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Separatedness: a section is determined by its restrictions to a cover]
  \label{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.UniqueGluing}).}
  Let \(F\) be a sheaf on a topological space, \(V\) an open, and \(\{U_i\}\) a family of opens with
  \(\bigsqcup_i U_i = V\). If two sections \(s, t \in F(V)\) have equal restrictions
  \(s|_{U_i} = t|_{U_i}\) for every \(i\), then \(s = t\). This separatedness over a cover is the
  uniqueness half of the sheaf condition; it discharges the \(\mathrm{exists\_of\_eq}\) obligation
  (a global section vanishing on \(D(f)\) is killed by a power of \(f\)) and the final
  ``conclude on \(D(f)\)'' step of the cover-form descent
  (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}).
\end{lemma}

% --- (D) The section-localization descent: the gap1 keystone ---

\begin{lemma}[Section-localization descent from a cover hypothesis (gap1 keystone, D, cover form)]
  \label{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent_of_cover}
  \uses{lem:map_units_restrict_basicOpen, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
    lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib, lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}
  % NOTE: this is the LANDED, axiom-clean keystone (Lean decl
  % isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent_of_cover EXISTS). It takes the per-cover-element localization
  % data Hfr as a HYPOTHESIS and assembles all three IsLocalizedModule fields by pure finite-cover
  % sheaf theory; it does NOT route through the global QCoh ≃ Mod equivalence (= gap1 itself). The
  % NAMED form lem:section_localization_descent is this lemma instantiated at the quasi-coherence
  % cover once Hfr is produced (lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso).
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S "Sections over principal opens",
  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2152-L2171).
  % Cf. [Hartshorne], II.5.3.
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X$ be a scheme. Let $f \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
  %  Denote $X_f \subset X$ the open where $f$ is invertible, see
  %  Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-f-open}.
  %  If $X$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the canonical map
  %  $$
  %  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{O}_X)
  %  $$
  %  is an isomorphism. Moreover, if $\mathcal{F}$ is a quasi-coherent
  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules the map
  %  $$
  %  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})
  %  $$
  %  is an isomorphism."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S ``Sections over principal opens'',
  Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections}; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.5.3.}
  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and \(f \in R\). Suppose given a finite basic-open
  cover of \(\Spec R\): a finite set \(t \subseteq R\) with \(\mathrm{span}(t) = (1)\), so that
  \(\{D(r)\}_{r \in t}\) covers \(\Spec R\). Suppose moreover the \emph{per-piece localization data}
  \[
    \mathrm{Hfr} : \quad \text{for every open } U \le D(r) \text{ for some } r \in t, \quad
    \bigl(\Gamma(M, U) \longrightarrow \Gamma(M,\, D(f) \sqcap U)\bigr)
    \text{ is } \mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f),
  \]
  i.e.\ on every open contained in a cover member -- in particular each \(D(r)\) and each overlap
  \(D(r) \sqcap D(r')\) -- the restriction to the \(f\)-locus exhibits the target as the localization
  of the source at the powers of \(f\). Then the global section restriction
  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is itself \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\)
  over \(R\); that is, \(\Gamma(M, D(f)) \cong (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top)\).
\end{lemma}
% NOTE: the lemma STATEMENT is cited to Stacks lemma-invert-f-sections / Hartshorne II.5.3; the proof
% below is the project-internal finite-cover descent (gluing + separatedness + flatness of
% localization) that deliberately does NOT route through the global affine equivalence (which is gap1
% itself), so there is no transcribed `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF`.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:map_units_restrict_basicOpen, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
    lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib, lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}
  We verify the three fields of \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the restriction
  \(\theta : \Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\).

  \emph{\(\mathrm{map\_units}\).} For every \(n\) the element \(f^n\) acts invertibly on
  \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\): on the basic open \(D(f)\) the global section \(f\) is a unit of
  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}, D(f)) = R_f\), and scalar multiplication by a unit is bijective. This holds
  for an arbitrary sheaf of modules \(M\) and is \cref{lem:map_units_restrict_basicOpen}; it does not
  use \(\mathrm{Hfr}\).

  \emph{\(\mathrm{surj}\) (every section over \(D(f)\) is a fraction).} Let
  \(y \in \Gamma(M, D(f))\). On each cover member \(D(r)\), the restriction
  \(y|_{D(f) \sqcap D(r)}\) lies in the localization \(\Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(r))\) of
  \(\Gamma(M, D(r))\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) (the case \(U = D(r)\) of \(\mathrm{Hfr}\)), so there
  are \(x_r \in \Gamma(M, D(r))\) and \(n_r\) with \(f^{n_r}\, y|_{D(f)\sqcap D(r)} = x_r|_{D(f)\sqcap
  D(r)}\). Taking a common exponent \(N \ge \max_r n_r\) (the cover is finite) and rescaling, we may
  assume \(f^{N} y\) is represented on each \(D(r)\) by a section \(\tilde x_r\). On an overlap
  \(D(r) \sqcap D(r')\) the two representatives \(\tilde x_r, \tilde x_{r'}\) restrict to sections of
  \(\Gamma(M, D(r) \sqcap D(r'))\) that agree after further multiplication by \(f\): both compute
  \(f^{N} y\) on \(D(f) \sqcap D(r) \sqcap D(r')\), and the overlap restriction
  \(\Gamma(M, D(r)\sqcap D(r')) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(r) \sqcap D(r'))\) is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) (the case \(U = D(r) \sqcap D(r')\) of
  \(\mathrm{Hfr}\)), hence injective up to a power of \(f\); so there is a common further exponent
  \(P\) with \(f^{P}\tilde x_r\) and \(f^{P}\tilde x_{r'}\) equal on the overlap. The family
  \(\{f^{P}\tilde x_r\}_r\) is then compatible on overlaps and glues, by the sheaf condition over the
  finite cover (\cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}), to a global section
  \(x \in \Gamma(M, \top)\). Its restriction to \(D(f)\) equals \(f^{N+P} y\): the two agree on each
  \(D(f) \sqcap D(r)\), and \(\{D(f)\sqcap D(r)\}_r\) covers \(D(f)\), so they agree by separatedness
  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}). Thus \(\theta(x) = f^{N+P} y\), exhibiting \(y\) as the
  fraction \(x / f^{N+P}\).

  \emph{\(\mathrm{exists\_of\_eq}\) (a vanishing fraction is annihilated by a power of \(f\)).}
  Let \(x \in \Gamma(M, \top)\) with \(\theta(x) = x|_{D(f)} = 0\). On each cover member \(D(r)\), the
  restriction \(x|_{D(r)}\) maps to \(0\) in \(\Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(r))\), so by the localization
  hypothesis (the case \(U = D(r)\) of \(\mathrm{Hfr}\), which gives the \(\mathrm{exists\_of\_eq}\)
  field on that piece) there is \(n_r\) with \(f^{n_r}\, x|_{D(r)} = 0\). A common exponent
  \(N \ge \max_r n_r\) over the finite cover gives \(f^{N} x|_{D(r)} = 0\) for every \(r\); since the
  \(D(r)\) cover \(\Spec R\), separatedness (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) forces
  \(f^{N} x = 0\) in \(\Gamma(M, \top)\).

  These three fields are exactly \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for \(\theta\).
  Equivalently the comparison \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is an
  isomorphism -- the \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained from local data over a
  finite cover by sheaf gluing and separatedness, with the finite limit interacting with localization
  through the flatness of localization (\cref{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib},
  \cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}), never through the global affine equivalence.
\end{proof}

% --- (D, section transport) The Hfr ingredient: pullback-along-open-immersion section comparison ---

\begin{lemma}[Global sections of a pullback along an open immersion are sections over the image
  (gap1, Hfr section transport)]
  \label{lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gammaPullbackTopIso}
  \uses{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl gammaPullbackTopIso does NOT yet exist; it is the iter-036 prover
  % target — the SECTION analogue of P1's OBJECT-level transport
  % (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), Mathlib-absent at the pinned commit.
  % Once it lands, the named-form descent (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}) and gap1
  % (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) are both one-liners. Project-bespoke infrastructure:
  % no external verbatim source for the construction; it stands on its sketch.
  \textit{Project-bespoke infrastructure: the section-level transport feeding the per-cover-element
  hypothesis \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) of \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}.}
  Let \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules
  on \(X\). The global sections of the pullback module along \(j\) are canonically isomorphic to the
  sections of \(M\) over the image of \(j\):
  \[
    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M,\ \top\bigr)
    \;\cong\; \Gamma\bigl(M,\ \mathrm{range}\,j\bigr),
  \]
  naturally in the open argument: for opens \(V \le \top\) of \(U\) the comparison
  \(\Gamma((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M, V) \cong \Gamma(M, j(V))\) intertwines the
  restriction maps of the two presheaves, so any two such instances commute with the restriction
  between them.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
  For an open immersion \(j\), Mathlib's restriction functor \(j^{\mathrm{res}}\) satisfies
  \(\Gamma(j^{\mathrm{res}} M, V) = \Gamma(M, j(V))\) \emph{definitionally}
  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}); in particular at \(V = \top\) the right-hand side is
  \(\Gamma(M, j(\top)) = \Gamma(M, \mathrm{range}\,j)\). The restriction functor is naturally
  isomorphic to the universal-property pullback,
  \(j^{\mathrm{res}} \cong j^{*} = \mathrm{pullback}\,j\)
  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib},
  \cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}). Applying the global-sections functor \(\Gamma(-, V)\) to the
  component of this natural isomorphism at \(M\) gives, for each open \(V\), an isomorphism
  \(\Gamma((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M, V) \cong \Gamma(j^{\mathrm{res}} M, V)
  = \Gamma(M, j(V))\); naturality of the functor isomorphism in \(V\) makes these commute with the
  presheaf restriction maps. Taking \(V = \top\) yields the displayed isomorphism with
  \(\Gamma(M, \mathrm{range}\,j)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,

    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  Refine the quasi-coherence cover to a finite basic-open cover \(\{D(r_j)\}_{j=1}^m\) of \(\Spec R\)
  with each \(D(r_j) \le q.X_{i(j)}\)
  (\cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}). On each \(D(r_j)\) the restricted
  counit is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), so
  \(M|_{D(r_j)} \cong \widetilde{N_j}\) with \(N_j = \Gamma(M, D(r_j))\) a module over \(R_{r_j}\),
  and the basic-open sections of \(M\) below \(D(r_j)\) are the corresponding module localizations
  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}).

  Fix \(f \in R\). The sheaf condition for \(M\) over the finite cover \(\{D(r_j)\}\) and its pairwise
  intersections presents the global sections as the finite equalizer
  \[
    \Gamma(M, \top) \;=\; \mathrm{eq}\Bigl(\textstyle\prod_j \Gamma(M, D(r_j))
      \;\rightrightarrows\; \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(M, D(r_jr_k))\Bigr),
  \]
  the Mayer--Vietoris / unique-gluing description of sections
  (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}, \cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}); the same holds
  with \(\top\) replaced by \(D(f)\) and the cover by \(\{D(fr_j)\}\). Localizing at
  \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) is exact and commutes with this finite limit because localization is flat
  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}). On each cover piece the localization of
  \(\Gamma(M, D(r_j))\) away from \(f\) is \(\Gamma(M, D(fr_j)) = \Gamma(M|_{D(r_j)}, D(f))\), a
  localization of the tilde \(\widetilde{N_j}\) computed by
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}; the same on the overlaps. Hence the localized equalizer
  computes \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\), and the comparison map
  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is an isomorphism: the section
  restriction is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\). This is the
  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained directly from
  local-tilde data without appeal to the global affine equivalence.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover, lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso,

    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  Fix \(f \in R\). Refine the quasi-coherence cover to a finite basic-open cover
  \(\{D(r)\}_{r \in t}\) of \(\Spec R\), \(\mathrm{span}(t) = (1)\), with each \(D(r) \le q.X_{i(r)}\)
  (\cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}). It suffices to produce the
  per-cover-element localization data \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) of
  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}: for every open \(U \le D(r)\) (\(r \in t\)) the
  restriction \(\Gamma(M, U) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap U)\) is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\). The keystone then assembles the three fields of
  the conclusion for the global restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\).

  We obtain \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) at \(U \le D(r)\) from the per-element P1 transport. By
  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} the module \(M'\) obtained by restricting
  \(M\) to \(D(r)\) -- realised as the iterated pullback of \(M\) along \(\iota_{q.X_i}\), the
  basic-open immersion, and the affine identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) -- has an isomorphism
  \(\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) counit, so by
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} its section restriction to any basic open
  of \(\Spec R_r\) is a localization. The section transport \cref{lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso}, chained
  through the same three pullbacks, identifies \(\Gamma(M', \top) \cong \Gamma(M, D(r))\) and, at the
  \(f\)-locus, \(\Gamma(M', D(f')) \cong \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(r))\) (with \(f'\) the image of \(f\)
  in \(R_r\)), intertwining the restriction maps; the same for an overlap \(U = D(r) \sqcap D(r')\).
  Transporting the localization statement across these section isomorphisms gives that
  \(\Gamma(M, U) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap U)\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\),
  which is exactly \(\mathrm{Hfr}\).

  Feeding \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) and the cover \(\{D(r)\}_{r\in t}\) to
  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover} yields that
  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over
  \(R\), i.e.\ \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top) \cong \Gamma(M, D(f))\). This is the
  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained from local-tilde data
  over a finite cover without appeal to the global affine equivalence.
\end{proof}
